title: Galerkin-finite difference method for fractional parabolic partial differential equations
author: Hossan, Datta, et al.
materials: And we are going to use, |Rmn|≤C(δτ)where,n=1,2,...,Nandm=1,2,...,M−1
Here, Φ1(x)=x2−x, Φ2(x)=x2−3x22+x3. Hence, we obtain,
The exact solution for this problem is, u(x,t)=x2(x−1)t(2+α)

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We solve the Eq. (3) with the help of the Galerkin Weighted Residual method. In this method, basis functions need to meet the homogeneous boundary requirements. And initial condition is another important step in solving any kind of partial differential equation. In this case, we select initially a trial solution. Let us consider such an approximate solution of the Eq. (3) as(5)u˜(x,τ)=Φ0(x,τ)+∑j=1naj(τ)Φj(x) where Φ0(x,τ) satisfies the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions, Φj(x) denotes the basis functions which must fulfill the homogeneous boundary requirements, and the unknown parameters, aj(τ) are function of time [44].

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Now the residual function is(6)R(x,τ)=∂u˜∂τ−q(x)∂βu˜∂xβ−s(x,τ)Substituting the Eq. (5) into (6) we get,(7)∂u˜∂τ=∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτΦj(x)+∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τand(8)∂βu˜∂xβ=∑j=1naj(τ)dβΦj(x)dxβ+∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβThen the weighted residual equation is(9)∫01R(x,τ)Φi(x)dx=0where, Φi(x) are the basis functions defined in (2) for all i=1,2,...,n.

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Substituting (7) and (8) into Eq. (6), then (9) leads us,(10)∫01(∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτΦj(x)+∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τ−q(x)(∑j=1naj(τ)dβΦj(x)dxβ+∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβ)−s(x,τ))Φi(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin method for space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: Now(11)∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτ∫01Φj(x)Φi(x)dx+∫01∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τΦi(x)dx−∑j=1naj(τ)∫01(dβΦj(x)dxβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx−∫01(∂βϕ0(x,τ)∂xβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx−∫01s(x,τ)Φi(x)dx=0 which can be rewritten as(12)∑j=1ndaj(τ)dτ∫01Φj(x)Φi(x)dx−∑j=1naj(τ)∫01(dβΦj(x)dxβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx=∫01(∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβ)Φi(x)q(x)dx−∫01∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τΦi(x)dx+∫01s(x,τ)Φi(x)dxWe can define the integrals of the above equation in terms of matrix. The dimension of the matrix depends on the number of parameters or basis functions. In matrix notation, the Eq. (12) reduces to(13)[C]{da(τ)dτ}+[K]{a(τ)}={F}where,Cij=∫01Φj(x)Φi(x)dxKij=−∫01(∂β∂xβΦj(x))Φi(x)q(x)dxFi=∫01(∂βΦ0(x,τ)∂xβq(x)−∂Φ0(x,τ)∂τ+s(x,τ))Φi(x)dx

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: This method is a combination of discretization of time fractional terms [34], and the modified Galerkin method for space fractional terms. Now we are ready to provide the mathematical formulation for the Eq. (4) explicitly. For this, we introduce the discretized approximation of the time-fractional derivative [34] as:∂αu(x,τn+1)∂τα=(δτ)−αΓ(2−α){u(x,τn+1)−u(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u(x,τn+1−l)−u(x,τn−l))((l+1)(1−α)−l(1−α))} which is equivalent to,(14)∂αu(x,τn+1)∂τα=V[u(x,τn+1)−u(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u(x,τn+1−l)−u(x,τn−l))Pl]where, Pl=((l+1)(1−α)−l(1−α)), V=(δτ)−αΓ(2−α) and time step size as, δτ=τN

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: After applying Galerkin method, we calculate u(x,τn) at different values of x, and at any specified time step, τn which is defined for n=1,2,...,N.

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We choose the approximate solution as,(15)u˜(x,τn)=Φ0(x,τn)+∑i=1kai(τn)Φi(x)where, j represents the number of parameters. Unlike as Galerkin method, Φ0(x,τn) calculates the non-homogeneous part of the boundary conditions at any specified value of time, τn,ai(τn) are the unknown parameters whose values are needed to be determined again at the specified time, τn and Φi(x) are basis functions such as we have discussed in (2). Now we define the residual function asR(x,τn+1)=V[u˜(x,τn+1)−u˜(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u˜(x,τn+1−l)−u˜(x,τn−l))Pl]−q(x)[∂βu˜(x,τn+1)∂xβ]−s(x,τn+1)To find a set of linear equations, we consider:(16)∫01R(x,τn+1)Φj(x)dx=0wherej=1,2,...,kor equivalently,(17)∫01(V[u˜(x,τn+1)−u˜(x,τn)+∑l=1n(u˜(x,τn+1−l)−u˜(x,τn−l))Pl]−q(x)[∂βu˜(x,τn+1)∂xβ]−s(x,τn+1))Φj(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: After substitution of u˜ from Eq. (15) into Eq. (17) we get,∫01(V[(Φ0(x,τn+1)+∑i=1kai(τn+1)Φi(x))−(Φ0(x,τn)+∑i=1kai(τn)Φi(x))+∑l=1n((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)+∑i=1kai(τn+1−l)Φi(x))−(Φ0(x,τn−l)+∑i=1kai(τn−l)Φi(x)))Pl]−q(x)[∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1)+∑i=1kai(τn+1)Φi(x))∂xβ]−s(x,τn+1))Φj(x)dx=0or,V[∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1)+∑i=1kai(τn+1)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx−∫01((Φ0(x,τn)+∑i=1kai(τn)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx+∑l=1n(∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)+∑i=1kai(τn+1−l)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx−∫01((Φ0(x,τn−l)+∑i=1kai(τn−l)Φi(x))Φj(x))dx)Pl]−∫01q(x)[∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1)∂xβ+∑i=1kai(τn+1)∂β(Φi(x))∂xβ]Φj(x)dx−∫01s(x,τn+1)Φj(x)dx=0 or,(18)V[∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1)−Φ0(x,τn))Φj(x)dx+∑i=1k(ai(τn+1)−ai(τn))∫01Φi(x)Φj(x)dx+∑l=1n(∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)−Φ0(x,τn−l))Φj(x)dx+∑i=1k(ai(τn+1−l)−ai(τn−l))×∫01Φi(x)Φj(x)dx)Pl]−∫01q(x)(∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1))∂xβ)Φj(x)dx−∑i=1kai(τn+1)×∫01q(x)∂β(Φi(x))∂xβΦj(x)dx−∫01s(x,τn+1)Φj(x)dx=0

steps.title: Galerkin-Finite Difference method for time-space fractional PDE
steps.instruction: We evaluate the values of unknown parameters, ai(τn) after substituting the value of n in Eq. (18). For example, if we substitute n=0, we obtain linear equations at τ=δτ. Solving those equations we find out an approximate solution at a specified time value as a function of x such as u˜(x,δτ). Repeating the steps for n=1,2,...,N, we will be able to calculate the values of u(x,τ) at different x for any value of time. We express the integrals in terms of matrix and in matrix notation,(19)V[[G]{a(τn+1)−a(τn)}+∑l=1n({H}+[G]{a(τn+1−l)−a(τn−l)})Pl]=[K]{a(τn+1)}+{M}whereGij=∫01Φi(x)Φj(x)dxHj=∫01((Φ0(x,τn+1−l)−Φ0(x,τn−l))Φj(x)dxKij=∫01q(x)(∂β∂xβΦi(x))Φj(x)dxMj=∫01(q(x)∂β(Φ0(x,τn+1))∂xβ−V(Φ0(x,τn+1)−Φ0(x,τn))+s(x,τn+1))Φj(x)dx

steps.title: reduces to(29)V[Em2−Em1+(Em1−Em0)p1)]=K∂β∂xβ(Em2)
steps.instruction: reduces to(29)V[Em2−Em1+(Em1−Em0)p1)]=K∂β∂xβ(Em2).Using the notation ∥E2∥∞=|Ej2|, we obtain.(30)∥E2∥∞=|Ej2|≤|Ej2|−KV(∂β∂xβ|Ej2|)=|(1−p1)Ej1+p1Ej0|≤(1−p1)|Ej0|+p1|Ej0|≤∥E0∥∞Similarly, by using method of induction we can show that for any l-th time step,(31)∥El+1∥∞≤∥E0∥∞Thus, we may conclude that the finite difference of the time fractional term is unconditionally stable, and details are available in [34].

description: We provide the first Bernoulli polynomials and their modified versions to use as basis functions for the weighted residual methods throughout this paper.
